Add type checking to my Python package
expected_behavior: inspects the repo (or asks) for framework dependencies like Django, SQLAlchemy, or Pydantic and for src vs flat layout before recommending a checker, and presents the mypy vs pyright vs ty choice as unsettled trade-offs rather than declaring one winner
writes checker config into pyproject.toml with strictness matched to project age — strict for a new project, a documented ratchet plan for a legacy one
adds the checker as a pinned dev dependency and runs it via uv (noting a plain pip fallback), then fixes or triages the reported errors
for a library, adds py.typed and configures the build backend to include it
does not quote specific speed multipliers or conformance percentages as established facts

cases.prompt: mypy --strict reports 400 errors on our legacy codebase, roll it out gradually
expected_behavior: proposes a per-module ratchet (global strict with [[tool.mypy.overrides]] exempting legacy modules, or staged flag adoption) instead of a big-bang fix or abandoning strict
warns that global strict plus per-module rule downgrades do not compose cleanly and shows working override config (ignore_errors for legacy modules, ignore_missing_imports for untyped deps)
recommends error-code-scoped ignores like '# type: ignore[arg-type]' with warn_unused_ignores enabled, not bare ignores
defines the ratchet direction — the override/exemption list only shrinks over time and is tracked as debt

cases.prompt: Downstream users of our library say all our types show up as Any in their editor
expected_behavior: diagnoses a missing or unshipped py.typed marker (PEP 561) as the likely cause
adds py.typed inside the import package and adds the build-backend include config the backend needs (e.g. setuptools package-data)
builds the wheel and verifies py.typed is actually inside the built artifact, not just the source tree
recommends checking the public API with both mypy and pyright since downstream consumers use both

cases.prompt: pyright strict keeps flagging our Pydantic model constructors as errors
expected_behavior: explains the root cause — static checkers cannot see through Pydantic before/wrap validators and coercion, so constructor calls with pre-validation input types look wrong statically
presents options with trade-offs (mypy with the pydantic.mypy plugin, rule-scoped pyright ignores, relaxing specific rules for model modules) instead of one blanket fix
warns against blanket '# type: ignore' and against assuming a statically clean model is runtime-safe, since Pydantic coerces by default unless its own strict mode is enabled
does not add a second type checker as a CI gate without flagging the dual suppression-comment cost

cases.prompt: should I use mypy, pyright, or ty?
expected_behavior: frames the checker race as genuinely unsettled and maps situations to checkers — plugin-dependent stacks to mypy, new projects or editor-first teams to pyright/basedpyright, risk-tolerant uv shops to a ty pilot, huge monorepos to evaluating Pyrefly
flags ty and Pyrefly maturity caveats, including the absence of a plugin system for Django/SQLAlchemy/Pydantic stacks
avoids quoting benchmark multipliers or conformance percentages as fact
recommends committing to one primary CI checker and warns that running two means maintaining parallel suppression comments
